narrative:
On July 1, 2026, the Karnataka High Court recorded a hearing rate of 42.8%, with 1,031 cases heard out of 2,407 scheduled. This performance represents a marginal increase from the previous day’s rate of 41.2% (June 30). The data indicates a disparity in hearing rates between litigants: commoners saw a 44.5% hearing rate, while government-related matters were heard at a rate of 37.5%, resulting in a bias gap of -7.0%.

Judge-wise performance varied significantly across the 45 presiding judges. High-clearance activity was observed in Hall 20 (Shivashankar Amarannavar) and Hall 32 (Vijaykumar A. Patil), both of which achieved hearing rates exceeding 80% for their respective dockets. Conversely, several halls, including Hall 6 and Hall 15, recorded sessions with zero heard matters, reflecting the operational variability of the daily bench.

Regarding hearing stages, the court prioritized "Admission" and "Orders" stages, which achieved hearing rates of 51.6% and 45.5%, respectively. While "Preliminary Hearings" maintained a conversion rate of 42.4%, substantive "Final Hearings" remained challenging, with only 21.8% of the 418 scheduled cases reaching a hearing stage. This aligns with a broader trend where procedural stages continue to dominate the daily docket over final disposals.

Case-type distribution remains concentrated in Writ Petitions (WP) and Criminal Petitions (CRL.P), which accounted for over 1,400 of the total scheduled matters. While WPs showed a moderate hearing rate of 36.6%, Miscellaneous First Appeals (MFA) and Criminal Revision Petitions (CRL.RP) demonstrated higher throughput, with hearing rates of 58.6% and 62.1% respectively. Overall, the clearance patterns on July 1 remained consistent with the efficiency levels observed throughout the preceding week.
